content.text: Overview

Every agentic system sits somewhere on a spectrum between full human control and full autonomy. Full control is safe but slow — every action waits for a human reviewer. Full autonomy is fast but risky — the agent acts without any check. The practical goal is not to maximize autonomy but to place each action type at the right point on the spectrum: autonomous where the risk and error rate are low, controlled where they are not. This calibration is done along three independent dimensions: permission scope, action depth, and reversibility.

attrs.alt: The three control surfaces — permission scope, action depth, and reversibility — each independently tunable to set the autonomy level for an action type

content.text: The Fundamental Tension

Autonomy and control trade against each other across four dimensions simultaneously. Understanding what you are giving up in each dimension is what makes the design decision explicit rather than implicit.
Dimension
More Control Gives You
More Autonomy Gives You
Speed
Catch errors before they propagate
Throughput limited only by compute
Scale
Every action reviewed; errors caught individually
Handle millions of actions per day without reviewers
Accountability
A human made or approved every decision
Agent traces provide audit trail, but no human sign-off
User trust
Users see exactly what the agent proposes before it acts
Faster for user; invisible until something goes wrong
Control Surface 1: Permission Scope

Permission scope is the set of resources the agent can read and the set of actions it can execute. A narrow scope limits the blast radius of a mistake — if an agent with read-only access to one calendar makes an error, it cannot touch your email or your files. Enforce scope at the tool definition level, not in prompt instructions: an LLM instructed not to do something will still try if prompted; a tool that does not exist cannot be called.

# Scope enforcement: give the agent only the tools it needs for this task
import anthropic
from typing import Literal

# Full tool set available in the system
ALL_TOOLS = ["read_email", "send_email", "read_calendar", "write_calendar",
             "read_crm", "write_crm", "execute_sql", "call_external_api"]

# Scope profiles — narrow to wide
SCOPE_PROFILES: dict[str, list[str]] = {
    "read_only":     ["read_email", "read_calendar", "read_crm"],
    "calendar_only": ["read_calendar", "write_calendar"],
    "full_inbox":    ["read_email", "send_email", "read_calendar"],
    "full_access":   ALL_TOOLS,
}

# Tool schemas (abbreviated for clarity)
TOOL_SCHEMAS: dict[str, dict] = {
    "read_email":    {"name": "read_email",    "description": "Read emails (read-only)",
                     "input_schema": {"type": "object", "properties": {"query": {"type": "string"}}, "required": ["query"]}},
    "send_email":    {"name": "send_email",    "description": "Send email to recipient",
                     "input_schema": {"type": "object", "properties": {"to": {"type": "string"}, "body": {"type": "string"}}, "required": ["to", "body"]}},
    "read_calendar": {"name": "read_calendar", "description": "Read calendar events",
                     "input_schema": {"type": "object", "properties": {"date": {"type": "string"}}, "required": ["date"]}},
    "write_calendar":{"name": "write_calendar","description": "Create or modify calendar event",
                     "input_schema": {"type": "object", "properties": {"event": {"type": "object"}}, "required": ["event"]}},
}

def make_agent(scope: Literal["read_only", "calendar_only", "full_inbox", "full_access"]):
    """Return an agent pre-configured with only the tools for the given scope."""
    allowed = SCOPE_PROFILES[scope]
    tools = [TOOL_SCHEMAS[t] for t in allowed if t in TOOL_SCHEMAS]

    client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    def run(task: str) -> str:
        messages = [{"role": "user", "content": task}]
        while True:
            response = client.messages.create(
                model="claude-opus-4-8",
                max_tokens=1024,
                tools=tools,   # agent literally cannot call tools outside this list
                messages=messages,
            )
            if response.stop_reason == "end_turn":
                return next(b.text for b in response.content if b.type == "text")
            messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content})
            messages.append({"role": "user", "content": [
                {"type": "tool_result", "tool_use_id": b.id, "content": "[result]"}
                for b in response.content if b.type == "tool_use"
            ]})
    return run

# Calendar assistant: can only read/write calendar — no email, no CRM
assistant = make_agent("calendar_only")
# Email summariser: can read email but NOT send it
summariser = make_agent("read_only")
Control Surface 2: Action Depth

Action depth is how many consecutive steps the agent takes before a human checkpoint. A depth of 1 means the agent proposes one action, the human reviews, and the agent executes; a depth of 10 means the agent plans and executes 10 steps before any review. Lower depth is safer but adds review overhead proportional to the number of actions; higher depth is efficient but compounds errors across steps before they are caught.

The right depth depends on the error independence of the steps. If each step is independent (read email, read another email, summarise), errors do not compound and higher depth is acceptable. If steps are dependent (read email → decide recipient → send reply), an error in step 2 propagates into step 3 — keep depth low.

# Depth-bounded agentic loop: checkpoint every N tool calls
import anthropic, json
from typing import Callable, Awaitable

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def run_with_depth_gate(
    task: str,
    tools: list[dict],
    checkpoint: Callable[[list[dict]], bool],  # returns True = continue, False = abort
    max_depth: int = 3,                        # how many tool calls before checkpoint
) -> str:
    """
    Run an agent with a human checkpoint every  tool calls.
     receives the list of tool calls made since the last checkpoint
    and returns whether to continue.
    """
    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": task}]
    pending_calls: list[dict] = []

    while True:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-8",
            max_tokens=2048,
            tools=tools,
            messages=messages,
        )
        messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content})

        if response.stop_reason == "end_turn":
            return next(b.text for b in response.content if b.type == "text")

        tool_results = []
        for block in response.content:
            if block.type != "tool_use":
                continue
            pending_calls.append({"name": block.name, "input": block.input})

            # Checkpoint when depth limit is reached
            if len(pending_calls) >= max_depth:
                if not checkpoint(pending_calls):
                    return "Task aborted by human reviewer at depth checkpoint."
                pending_calls = []   # reset counter after approved batch

            # Execute approved tool
            result = execute_tool(block.name, block.input)
            tool_results.append({
                "type": "tool_result",
                "tool_use_id": block.id,
                "content": json.dumps(result),
            })

        messages.append({"role": "user", "content": tool_results})

def execute_tool(name: str, args: dict) -> dict:
    # Placeholder
    return {"status": "ok"}

# Example: require a checkpoint every 2 tool calls
def my_checkpoint(calls: list[dict]) -> bool:
    print(f"[REVIEW] Agent is about to execute {len(calls)} action(s):")
    for c in calls:
        print(f"  {c['name']}({c['input']})")
    answer = input("Approve? [y/N] ").strip().lower()
    return answer == "y"
Control Surface 3: Reversibility

Reversibility is whether an action can be undone after it executes. It is the most important of the three control surfaces because it determines whether a mistake can be corrected. A reversible mistake is an inconvenience; an irreversible one is a liability. Design actions to be reversible wherever possible — stage changes in a draft state before committing, write to an append-only log before overwriting, and keep a rollback record for every destructive operation.

# Reversibility wrapper: make irreversible actions reversible via staging
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any, Callable
import uuid, json, datetime

@dataclass
class StagedAction:
    action_id:   str
    name:        str
    payload:     dict[str, Any]
    staged_at:   str
    committed:   bool = False
    rolled_back: bool = False

_stage: dict[str, StagedAction] = {}

def stage(name: str, payload: dict) -> StagedAction:
    """Stage an action without executing it. Returns a handle for commit/rollback."""
    action = StagedAction(
        action_id=str(uuid.uuid4()),
        name=name,
        payload=payload,
        staged_at=datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
    )
    _stage[action.action_id] = action
    return action

def commit(action_id: str, executor: Callable[[str, dict], Any]) -> Any:
    """Execute the staged action for real."""
    action = _stage[action_id]
    if action.committed or action.rolled_back:
        raise ValueError(f"{action_id} already finalised")
    result = executor(action.name, action.payload)
    action.committed = True
    return result

def rollback(action_id: str) -> None:
    """Cancel the staged action without executing it."""
    action = _stage[action_id]
    action.rolled_back = True

# Usage in an agent loop:
# 1. Agent proposes send_email → we call stage("send_email", {...})
# 2. Human reviews the staged action
# 3. Human approves → commit(action_id, real_send_email)
#    Human rejects  → rollback(action_id) (nothing was sent)

# For truly irreversible operations, always use a dry-run path first:
def send_email_dry_run(to: str, subject: str, body: str) -> dict:
    """Returns what would be sent without sending — show this to the reviewer."""
    return {"dry_run": True, "to": to, "subject": subject,
            "preview": body[:200] + ("..." if len(body) > 200 else "")}
The AutonomyConfig: Combining All Three Surfaces

In practice, each action type gets its own configuration that combines all three control surfaces into a single policy object. This makes the autonomy decision explicit and reviewable, and it can be updated independently per action type as trust accumulates.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal

OversightMode = Literal["hitl", "hotl", "auto"]

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class AutonomyConfig:
    """Autonomy policy for a single action type."""
    scope:       list[str]     # allowed tool names
    max_depth:   int           # tool calls before checkpoint (0 = require approval each call)
    reversible:  bool          # can the action be undone?
    oversight:   OversightMode # hitl | hotl | auto

    def requires_approval(self) -> bool:
        return self.oversight == "hitl"

    def requires_monitoring(self) -> bool:
        return self.oversight in ("hitl", "hotl")

# Per-action-type configuration
ACTION_CONFIGS: dict[str, AutonomyConfig] = {
    "summarise_inbox": AutonomyConfig(
        scope=["read_email"],
        max_depth=10,       # can read many emails without checkpoint
        reversible=True,    # reading is always reversible
        oversight="auto",
    ),
    "draft_reply": AutonomyConfig(
        scope=["read_email", "write_draft"],
        max_depth=3,
        reversible=True,    # drafts can be deleted
        oversight="hotl",   # monitor but do not block
    ),
    "send_reply": AutonomyConfig(
        scope=["read_email", "send_email"],
        max_depth=1,        # one action, then checkpoint
        reversible=False,   # sent email cannot be unsent
        oversight="hitl",   # require explicit approval
    ),
    "book_meeting": AutonomyConfig(
        scope=["read_calendar", "write_calendar"],
        max_depth=2,
        reversible=True,    # calendar events can be deleted
        oversight="hotl",
    ),
}

def check_action(action_type: str, tool_name: str) -> tuple[bool, str]:
    """Return (allowed, reason) for an action before execution."""
    config = ACTION_CONFIGS.get(action_type)
    if not config:
        return False, f"No policy for action type '{action_type}'"
    if tool_name not in config.scope:
        return False, f"Tool '{tool_name}' is outside the allowed scope for '{action_type}'"
    if not config.reversible and config.oversight == "auto":
        return False, "Irreversible action cannot be set to auto oversight"
    return True, "ok"

allowed, reason = check_action("send_reply", "send_email")
print(allowed, reason)   # True ok

allowed, reason = check_action("summarise_inbox", "send_email")
print(allowed, reason)   # False Tool 'send_email' is outside the allowed scope for 'summarise_inbox'
When to Increase Autonomy

Autonomy should increase in response to evidence, not intuition. The evidence that justifies loosening a control is: a sufficient number of actions observed under tighter oversight, a rejection rate below your tolerance, and a review latency that shows reviewers are not catching real errors (they approve everything quickly). When all three are met, it is safe to reduce oversight by one step — HITL → HOTL → auto — on that action type alone.
Minimum sample: at least 50 reviewed actions of the same type before reducing oversight — small samples have wide confidence intervals on error rate
Rejection rate < 2%: if humans are rejecting 1 in 50 actions, the agent makes too many mistakes to run unsupervised
Reduce one step at a time: skip from HITL directly to auto and you lose your safety net for an action type that has never been tested at HOTL level
Never promote irreversible actions past HOTL without a rollback mechanism: even a high approval rate does not justify full autonomy on actions that cannot be undone
Monitor for distribution shift: an agent that was accurate last month may not be accurate today if the environment changed — re-run the sample collection periodically
Autonomy Anti-Patterns
Blanket autonomy for a whole agent: granting full access to all tools because the agent is accurate on the common path ignores rare failure modes on uncommon paths
Scope by prompt, not by tool list: telling the agent "do not touch the billing API" in a system prompt gives no enforcement — remove the billing tools from the tool list entirely
Depth without checkpoints: an N-step chain where each step's output is the next step's input means one misclassification in step 1 corrupts all N subsequent steps
Promoting autonomy after a success run: three successful tasks in a row is not statistical evidence — you need 50+ on the same action type with no rejections
Autonomy without audit logging: if an autonomous agent makes a mistake that is not caught until later, you need full action traces to reconstruct what happened and why
Audit Logging for Autonomous Actions

When an action runs autonomously — without a human in the loop — the audit log is the only record that something happened. Every autonomous action must be logged with enough context to answer: what was requested, what the agent decided, what it executed, what the outcome was, and why it did not trigger a HITL gate.

import json, datetime, pathlib
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from typing import Any

@dataclass
class ActionLog:
    timestamp:     str
    action_type:   str
    tool_name:     str
    tool_input:    dict[str, Any]
    tool_output:   dict[str, Any]
    oversight:     str          # "hitl" | "hotl" | "auto"
    approved_by:   str | None   # reviewer id if HITL, None if auto
    execution_ms:  int

LOG_PATH = pathlib.Path("agent_audit.jsonl")

def log_action(entry: ActionLog) -> None:
    """Append one structured log entry per action."""
    with LOG_PATH.open("a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(asdict(entry)) + "
")

import time

def audited_execute(
    action_type: str,
    tool_name:   str,
    tool_input:  dict,
    executor:    Any,
    oversight:   str,
    approved_by: str | None = None,
) -> dict:
    start = time.monotonic()
    result = executor(tool_name, tool_input)
    elapsed_ms = int((time.monotonic() - start) * 1000)

    log_action(ActionLog(
        timestamp=datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
        action_type=action_type,
        tool_name=tool_name,
        tool_input=tool_input,
        tool_output=result,
        oversight=oversight,
        approved_by=approved_by,
        execution_ms=elapsed_ms,
    ))
    return result

# Query: find all auto-executed actions with errors (for post-hoc audit)
def find_auto_errors() -> list[dict]:
    entries = []
    with LOG_PATH.open() as f:
        for line in f:
            entry = json.loads(line)
            if entry["oversight"] == "auto" and entry["tool_output"].get("error"):
                entries.append(entry)
    return entries
Summary

Autonomy and control are not opposites to be traded off globally — they are tuned per action type across three independent surfaces: permission scope (what the agent can touch), action depth (how many steps before a checkpoint), and reversibility (whether mistakes can be undone). Start narrow on all three for every action type, measure outcomes under that tighter control, and relax each surface independently as the evidence justifies it. The pattern is always: enforce scope at the tool definition level, set depth to 1 for high-stakes dependent actions, require HITL for irreversible actions until reliability is proven, and log every autonomous action with enough context for a post-hoc audit.
